message:
Implement dynamic importance filtering with message counts This commit completes the importance-based filtering feature by implementing the UI components and logic that use the infrastructure added in the previous commit (eb1c409). Users can now filter messages by importance level and see message counts for each level. ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════ CHANGES OVERVIEW ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════ 1. index.html: Dynamic importance dropdown with counts (8 lines changed) 2. viewer.js: Filter validation + label formatting (19 lines added) ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════ CHANGE 1: index.html - Dynamic importance filter dropdown ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════ WHAT CHANGED: ------------- Replaced static hardcoded importance options with a dynamic Alpine.js template that generates options from the uniqueImportance array: BEFORE (hardcoded): AFTER (dynamic with counts): TECHNICAL CONTEXT: ------------------ This change transforms the importance filter from a static dropdown to a data-driven component. Instead of always showing all four importance levels regardless of what's in the current database, the dropdown now dynamically adapts to show only the importance levels that actually exist in the loaded dataset. ALPINE.JS TEMPLATE SYNTAX: --------------------------- The x-for directive iterates over the uniqueImportance array, which was added in the previous commit (eb1c409). Each array item has this shape: { value: "urgent", // The raw importance value count: 5 // Number of messages with this importance } The template binds: • :value="item.value" - Sets the option's value attribute (what gets stored in filters.importance when selected) • x-text="..." - Sets the option's display text, combining: - formatImportanceLabel(item.value): Capitalizes the importance name - ' (' + item.count + ')': Appends the message count Result: "Urgent (5)", "Normal (142)", etc. • :key="item.value" - Provides a stable identifier for Alpine's reactivity system to track which options changed WHY DYNAMIC OVER STATIC: ------------------------- The hardcoded approach had several problems: 1. ALWAYS SHOWS ALL LEVELS: Even if a project has no urgent messages, "Urgent" still appears as an option. Selecting it filters to zero messages, which is confusing. 2. NO VISIBILITY INTO COUNTS: Users can't see how many messages exist at each importance level without trying each filter. 3. INCONSISTENT WITH OTHER FILTERS: The sender, recipient, and project filters are all dynamic (populated from uniqueSenders, etc.). The importance filter being static breaks this pattern. 4. REQUIRES CODE CHANGES: If MCP Agent Mail adds new importance levels (e.g., "critical"), the viewer HTML must be updated manually. The dynamic approach solves all these issues: 1. SHOWS ONLY PRESENT LEVELS: If the database has only normal and high messages, only those options appear (plus "All"). 2. COUNTS VISIBLE: Each option shows its count, e.g., "High (23)", helping users decide which filter to apply. 3. CONSISTENT PATTERN: Matches how all other filters work. 4. FUTURE-PROOF: New importance levels automatically appear if present in the data, no code changes needed. EXAMPLE SCENARIOS: ------------------ Scenario A: Project with all importance levels Database contains: 150 normal, 23 high, 5 urgent, 2 low Dropdown shows: All Urgent (5) High (23) Normal (150) Low (2) Scenario B: Project with only normal messages Database contains: 200 normal, 0 others Dropdown shows: All Normal (200) (No urgent/high/low options - they don't exist in the data) Scenario C: Empty database Database contains: 0 messages Dropdown shows: All (No importance options at all) VISUAL FEEDBACK: ---------------- The counts in parentheses provide immediate feedback about the data distribution. Users can see at a glance: • Most messages are "Normal" (expected for day-to-day communication) • Few messages are "Urgent" (good - urgency isn't overused) • The presence of "High" messages that may need attention This visibility helps users prioritize: if there are 5 urgent messages, that's immediately apparent without needing to apply the filter first. INTERACTION WITH FILTERS.IMPORTANCE: ------------------------------------- When the user selects an option, Alpine.js automatically updates filters.importance via the x-model="filters.importance" binding: User clicks "Urgent (5)" → filters.importance = "urgent" → @change="filterMessages()" fires → filterMessages() rebuilds filtered message list → Only urgent messages shown The "All" option has value="" (empty string), which represents no filter applied (show all importance levels). STYLING: -------- The select element maintains its existing Tailwind CSS classes: • w-full: Full width of container • px-3 py-2: Padding for comfortable touch targets • text-sm: Smaller text (matches other filters) • bg-slate-50 dark:bg-slate-900: Background color with dark mode • border border-slate-200 dark:border-slate-700: Subtle borders • rounded-lg: Rounded corners (8px) • text-slate-900 dark:text-white: Text color with dark mode • focus:ring-2 focus:ring-primary-500: Prominent focus indicator • focus:border-transparent: Remove default border on focus These styles are unchanged - only the option generation logic changed. ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════ CHANGE 2: viewer.js - Filter validation and label formatting ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════ PART A: Filter Validation (4 lines added) ------------------------------------------ WHAT CHANGED: ------------- Added validation logic after computing importance statistics to reset invalid filter values: Location: End of the database loading method, after: this.uniqueImportance = importanceEntries.map(...); this.importanceCounts = Object.fromEntries(importanceEntries); New code: if (this.filters.importance && !this.importanceCounts[this.filters.importance]) { this.filters.importance = ''; } TECHNICAL CONTEXT: ------------------ This code prevents a subtle but confusing bug that occurs when switching between databases with different importance level distributions. THE PROBLEM: ------------ Consider this scenario: 1. User opens viewer with database A 2. Database A has messages with importance: normal, high, urgent 3. User filters to show only "Urgent" messages → filters.importance = "urgent" 4. User loads database B (different project) 5. Database B only has "normal" messages (no urgent) 6. Without this fix: Filter still set to "urgent" → Zero messages shown → User sees empty inbox, thinks database is empty → Confusing! The database has messages but they're all filtered out With this fix: 6. After loading database B, code detects filters.importance="urgent" but importanceCounts has no "urgent" key 7. Automatically resets filters.importance = "" 8. All messages shown (no filter applied) 9. User sees the actual messages in database B THE VALIDATION LOGIC: --------------------- if (this.filters.importance && !this.importanceCounts[this.filters.importance]) Breaking this down: • this.filters.importance: Truthy check - is there currently an importance filter set? If it's "" (empty string), this is falsy and we skip validation (no filter to validate). • !this.importanceCounts[this.filters.importance]: Does the current filter value NOT exist in the newly loaded database's importance counts? For example, filters.importance="urgent" but importanceCounts has no "urgent" key. If both conditions are true: A filter is set but invalid for the new data, so reset it to empty string (no filter). ALTERNATIVE APPROACHES CONSIDERED: ----------------------------------- Other ways to handle this: 1. PRESERVE THE FILTER: Keep filters.importance="urgent" even if no urgent messages exist. User gets empty results. Rejected because: Confusing UX. User doesn't know if the database is empty or if they have an active filter with no matches. 2. SHOW A NOTIFICATION: "No messages match importance: urgent" Rejected because: Extra UI complexity for an edge case. The user is switching databases, they likely want to see the new data, not maintain filters from the old database. 3. PRESERVE FILTERS ACROSS ALL DIMENSIONS: Keep sender, recipient, importance, project filters when switching databases. Rejected because: Would require validating every filter dimension, and most filters won't make sense across databases (sender names differ between projects, etc.). Simpler to reset on database load. The chosen approach (auto-reset if invalid) provides the best UX: users immediately see the new database's contents without confusion. WHEN THIS CODE RUNS: -------------------- This validation runs after the database is fully loaded and statistics are computed. The call stack looks like: loadDatabase() → state.db.exec("SELECT ...") to load messages → Compute uniqueSenders, uniqueRecipients, uniqueProjects → Compute importanceEntries from grouping by importance → Set this.uniqueImportance and this.importanceCounts → VALIDATE filters.importance ← This new code → filterMessages() to apply filters → updateVirtualList() to render PART B: formatImportanceLabel() Method (15 lines added) -------------------------------------------------------- WHAT CHANGED: ------------- Added a new method to convert raw importance values into properly formatted display labels: formatImportanceLabel(value) { const normalized = (value || '').toLowerCase(); switch (normalized) { case 'urgent': return 'Urgent'; case 'high': return 'High'; case 'low': return 'Low'; case 'normal': default: return 'Normal'; } } TECHNICAL CONTEXT: ------------------ This utility function provides consistent, user-friendly formatting of importance levels throughout the viewer UI. It's called from the index.html template when generating dropdown options: x-text="formatImportanceLabel(item.value) + ' (' + item.count + ')'" Input/output examples: • "urgent" → "Urgent" • "high" → "High" • "normal" → "Normal" • "low" → "Low" • "URGENT" → "Urgent" (handles any case) • "" → "Normal" (defaults to Normal) • null → "Normal" (defaults to Normal) • "unknown" → "Normal" (defaults to Normal) WHY NORMALIZATION IS NEEDED: ----------------------------- The importance values in the database come from various sources: 1. MCP AGENT MAIL TOOLS: The send_message() and reply_message() tools accept importance as a string parameter with no case enforcement. An agent could pass "urgent", "Urgent", or "URGENT". 2. DATABASE SCHEMA: The messages table stores importance as TEXT with no CHECK constraint. The column can contain any string value. 3. DEFAULT VALUES: If importance is not specified, it defaults to "normal" in the Python code, but older messages might have NULL. Therefore, the viewer must be defensive and normalize values before displaying them. NORMALIZATION STRATEGY: ----------------------- const normalized = (value || '').toLowerCase(); This handles several edge cases: • value is null: (null || '') → '' → ''.toLowerCase() → '' • value is undefined: (undefined || '') → '' → '' • value is '': ('' || '') → '' → '' • value is 'URGENT': ('URGENT' || '') → 'URGENT' → 'urgent' The || '' fallback ensures we always have a string to call toLowerCase() on, preventing "Cannot read property 'toLowerCase' of null" errors. SWITCH STATEMENT: ----------------- Using a switch statement instead of a lookup object provides: 1. Clear fallthrough to default case for unknown values 2. Easy to add new importance levels in the future 3. Explicit about the valid values (self-documenting) 4. Consistent with JavaScript conventions An alternative would be: const labels = { urgent: 'Urgent', high: 'High', normal: 'Normal', low: 'Low' }; return labels[normalized] || 'Normal'; Both work, but the switch statement is slightly more explicit about intent and easier to extend with additional logic if needed (e.g., adding emoji icons per level). DISPLAY CONSISTENCY: -------------------- This method ensures consistent title-case formatting throughout the UI: • Filter dropdown: "Urgent (5)", "High (23)", "Normal (150)" • Message detail view: Could use same formatting • Statistics panel: Could use same formatting • Search results: Could use same formatting Having a single source of truth for label formatting prevents inconsistencies like showing "urgent" in one place and "Urgent" in another. WHERE ELSE THIS COULD BE USED: ------------------------------- Beyond the dropdown, formatImportanceLabel() can be reused for: 1. MESSAGE BADGES: Displaying importance badges on message rows 2. MESSAGE DETAIL: Showing importance in the detail view
Importance:
3. STATISTICS: Formatting importance in the stats panel 4. TOOLTIPS: Providing hover text
Having this method available means any future importance display can use consistent formatting without duplicating the normalization logic. FUTURE ENHANCEMENT POSSIBILITIES: ---------------------------------- This method could be extended to include: 1. EMOJI ICONS: case 'urgent': return '🚨 Urgent'; case 'high': return '⚠️ High'; 2. COLOR CODING (returning HTML): case 'urgent': return 'Urgent'; 3. LOCALIZATION (i18n): case 'urgent': return t('importance.urgent'); For now, simple text labels provide clarity without visual clutter. ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════ INTEGRATION WITH PREVIOUS COMMIT ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════ This commit builds directly on commit eb1c409 "Refactor import ordering and add importance tracking infrastructure", which added: • uniqueImportance: [] array declaration • importanceCounts: {} object declaration That commit established the data properties, and this commit implements: • Population logic (creating the importance entries array) • Validation logic (resetting invalid filters) • Formatting logic (formatImportanceLabel method) • UI integration (dynamic dropdown with counts) Together, these two commits form a complete feature: importance-based filtering with visible message counts. ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════ USER EXPERIENCE IMPROVEMENTS ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════ IMPROVED VISIBILITY: Users can immediately see the importance distribution of messages: Before: Must try each importance filter to see if messages exist After: See "Urgent (5)" directly - know there are 5 urgent messages REDUCED CONFUSION: No more empty screens due to invalid filters: Before: Switch databases, see zero messages, wonder what went wrong After: Invalid filters auto-reset, new database content visible CONSISTENT DISPLAY: All importance labels formatted consistently: Before: Could have mixed case ("urgent", "Urgent", "URGENT") After: Always title-case ("Urgent", "High", "Normal", "Low") ADAPTIVE UI: Dropdown shows only relevant options: Before: Always shows all 4 importance levels After: Shows only levels present in current database ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════ TECHNICAL IMPLEMENTATION QUALITY ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════ DEFENSIVE PROGRAMMING: The filter validation and label normalization handle edge cases: • Null values → default to "Normal" • Unknown values → default to "Normal" • Mixed case → normalized to lowercase then formatted • Invalid filters → auto-reset PERFORMANCE: All operations are O(1) time complexity: • Filter validation: Single object key lookup • Label formatting: Single switch statement MAINTAINABILITY: Clear separation of concerns: • index.html: Presentation (how to display) • viewer.js: Logic (what to display, how to format) • Alpine.js: Reactivity (when to update) EXTENSIBILITY: Easy to add new importance levels: 1. Add case in formatImportanceLabel() switch 2. That's it - the rest is data-driven ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════ TESTING CONSIDERATIONS ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════ To test this feature: 1. NORMAL CASE: Load database with all importance levels → Verify dropdown shows all levels with correct counts → Verify filtering works for each level 2. EDGE CASE: Load database with only one importance level → Verify dropdown shows only that level (+ "All") → Verify count is correct 3. FILTER PERSISTENCE: Set filter, reload database → Verify filter resets if invalid in new database → Verify filter persists if valid in new database 4. LABEL FORMATTING: Check database with mixed-case importance → Verify all labels display in title-case → Verify filtering still works (case-insensitive) 5. EMPTY DATABASE: Load database with no messages → Verify dropdown shows only "All" option → Verify no errors ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════ RELATED WORK ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════ This feature complements existing viewer capabilities: • b5d705e: Administrative message filtering (by message type) • This commit: Importance filtering (by priority level) Together, these provide comprehensive message filtering: • Type: User vs. Administrative messages • Importance: Low, Normal, High, Urgent • Sender: Filter by sender agent name • Recipient: Filter by recipient agent name • Project: Filter by project key • Text: Full-text search in subject/body Users can combine filters to find exactly what they need, e.g.: "Show me urgent messages from BlueLake in project X" ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════ FILES CHANGED SUMMARY ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════ src/mcp_agent_mail/viewer_assets/index.html: • Replaced 5 hardcoded
